slides.title: A Different Canada, By Age
slides.statNote: PLACEHOLDER — replace with real StatCan/CMHC data
slides.body:
For much of the twentieth century, Canada promised each generation that hard work would produce a better life than the one before. Stable employment, accessible housing, and affordable education formed the foundation of what many Canadians saw as a reliable social contract.

For many young Canadians today, that contract appears increasingly fragile. Across the country, younger generations are entering adulthood facing housing prices far out of reach of their incomes, rising living costs, and higher levels of student debt than previous cohorts. While older generations often built wealth through homeownership during decades of rising asset prices, younger Canadians must navigate an economic environment where the entry barriers to wealth accumulation are dramatically higher.

This generational divide is not simply the result of bad luck or individual choices. It is deeply connected to political decisions about housing policy, taxation, labour markets, and social supports. Understanding the roots of this divide requires examining how policy choices and economic structures have evolved over time.
slides.question: If the country is richer, why does it feel harder?

slides.title: Housing Split the Generations
slides.statNote: PLACEHOLDER — replace with real StatCan/CMHC data
slides.body:
When shelter becomes a wealth engine, outcomes start depending on when you entered the market. Housing is widely considered the most significant contributor to generational economic inequality in Canada.
For much of the late twentieth century, housing prices grew gradually alongside incomes. Homeownership was broadly accessible to middle-class households, allowing many families to accumulate wealth as property values rose over time.
Beginning in the early 2000s, however, housing markets in major Canadian cities began to change dramatically.
Several factors contributed to this shift:
Limited housing supply in growing urban regions
Zoning regulations that restricted higher-density development
Rising demand driven by population growth and immigration
Low interest rates that increased borrowing capacity
Housing increasingly treated as an investment asset
The result has been a sharp increase in home prices relative to income.
In cities such as Toronto and Vancouver, housing prices have grown several times faster than wages over the past two decades. As a result, younger Canadians often face years of saving simply to accumulate a down payment.
For many, homeownership has shifted from a realistic expectation to a distant aspiration.
slides.question: Did timing become destiny?

slides.title: Rent Took the Slack
slides.statNote: PLACEHOLDER — replace with real StatCan/CMHC data
slides.body: Higher rent leaves less room for saving, schooling, and starting a family.
slides.question: What does 'adult life' cost now?

slides.title: Pay Didn't Keep Up
slides.statNote: PLACEHOLDER — replace with real StatCan/CMHC data
slides.body: Even small gaps compound: essentials rise, but paychecks lag.
slides.question: Is this a temporary spike—or the new baseline?

slides.title: The Starting Line Moved
slides.statNote: PLACEHOLDER — replace with real StatCan/CMHC data
slides.body: For many, adulthood begins with debt and delayed milestones.
slides.question: What happens when the 'first step' costs more?

slides.title: Policy Created Feedback Loops
slides.statNote: PLACEHOLDER — replace with real StatCan/CMHC data
slides.body: Once a policy creates winners, those winners gain influence—making the system harder to change.
slides.question: Can a policy ever truly be reversed?

slides.title: Power Is Split, So Is Blame
slides.statNote: PLACEHOLDER — replace with real StatCan/CMHC data
slides.body: Housing and affordability sit across federal, provincial, and municipal decisions—often misaligned.
slides.question: If responsibility is shared, is anyone accountable?

slides.title: People Feel the Trade-offs
slides.statNote: PLACEHOLDER — replace with real StatCan/CMHC data
slides.body: There are fixes—but each has costs, politics, and unintended consequences.
slides.question: What would you trade to make it fairer?

quotes.text: PLACEHOLDER QUOTE 1 — I graduated into a world where my parents' advice doesn't apply anymore.
PLACEHOLDER QUOTE 2 — Saving for a down payment feels like filling a bathtub with a teaspoon.
PLACEHOLDER QUOTE 3 — I got in just in time, but I worry about my kids constantly.
PLACEHOLDER QUOTE 4 — The ladder I climbed has had its lower rungs removed.
PLACEHOLDER QUOTE 5 — We didn't realize we were lucky until we saw what happened to the next generation.
PLACEHOLDER QUOTE 6 — The rules changed, and the young are paying the price.

ethicsNote: Quotes used with consent; anonymity available upon request.

policyOptions.title: Increase Housing Supply
policyOptions.description: Build more homes across all types—market, affordable, social housing.
policyOptions.tradeoff: Requires infrastructure investment and faces local pushback (NIMBYism).

policyOptions.title: Tax & Asset Reforms
policyOptions.description: Address speculation, foreign ownership, and capital gains exemptions.
policyOptions.tradeoff: Political backlash from existing homeowners and investors.

policyOptions.title: Income Supports
policyOptions.description: Expand rent assistance, housing benefits, and wage subsidies.
policyOptions.tradeoff: High fiscal cost and debates over targeting effectiveness.

policyOptions.title: Labour Market Measures
policyOptions.description: Raise minimum wages, strengthen worker protections, support unions.
policyOptions.tradeoff: Concerns about employer costs and potential inflation.
